author.name: Gary Sheng - The Applied AI Guy

text:
in the past month, i've had dozens of conversations about the future with people who are definitively on the frontier.

just wanted to share some of these insights with y'all, in no particular order:

1. the bottleneck to applied AI isn't technical skill. it's imagination. most

the biggest AI opportunity right now is mid-market companies ($5M-$50M+ revenue):  

• too big for cookie-cutter solutions 
• too small for enterprise consulting 
• drowning in manual workflows 
• have budget but zero AI expertise  

they’re stuck with serious cash but frozen

in 2026, 

every company will be looking for “ai nerds”

not data scientists. not ml engineers. just people who know how to automate boring shit with claude code, ai and llms

the new valuable skill is knowing which tasks to automate and how to glue tools together

go to grok and paste a profile url.   

ask it “scrape tweets above 1k likes about [keyword]” for any account in your niche.  

grab 5-10 of yours plus 5-10 from 3 top accounts in your space.  

now build a context profile:  

• paste all tweets into claude  
• ask it to find

Leaders have to rebuild their intuitions

"A lot of leaders have built intuitions over 10 or 15 years, and they are highly regarded for those intuitions. But now with AI in the picture, those intuitions will have to be relearned. And leaders have to be vulnerable to do that. 

I https://t.co/gsmp6ARL9O

the fastest way to learn AI automations is combining n8n's template library with a Claude project...

here's the system:

- go to n8n templates and filter by most recent
- download 10-15 workflow JSONs with their descriptions
- upload everything into a Claude project
- prompt
Jensen Huang: I want my engineers to stop coding. https://t.co/mpJvjmbCoR

this is fucking wild… 

nobody is selling claude-as-a-service to corporate boomers.. 

and it’s THE most obvious arbitrage play on the internet.

this demographic controls the majority of company budgets in the US. 

they have the authority to sign checks, the urgency to stay https://t.co/YQzWBgvTQF

Google co-founder Eric Schmidt's advice for making money in AI right now:  "Found an agentic AI company. Not one designing agents, build an agent to do something." 

He said this during a TED Talk in April 2025, almost one year ago.

https://t.co/t09Ko9sTTe
cloud apps have locked generations of people into self-limiting beliefs about what a computer can do
we're probably not far from a 1-person agency run by someone that lives in the terminal doing $100,000 per month with 4 clients, replacing entire teams with agents/workflows/skills

This is how to make money fast in 2026.

(he runs a $10M/year startup) https://t.co/Cy10ZIB2Nc

Stephen Wolfram says AI won't end work, but it will make learning low-level mechanics obsolete

We moved from assembler to high-level languages, removing the need for humans to understand the machine's plumbing

"you don't have to go to the lower levels... it's no longer useful https://t.co/JWpLWF4Tfs

Linus is Vibecoding now

It's literally over, they might vibe-code the Linux kernel now https://t.co/DhW5vT6Fo5

found a guy charging dentists $3,500/year to file paperwork

$2.4 million last year

zero followers

the glitch:

every dentist in america needs compliance documents filed or they get fined

osha stuff
hipaa updates
infection control logs

boring paperwork that changes every year

Mark Cuban’s advice to people trying to get into AI is simple. help businesses implement.

SMBs have budget but zero expertise. they’re not waiting for AGI. they’re waiting for someone to show them what’s possible.

what’s stopping you from being the one they call? https://t.co/NHoh41hnT3

Every individual AI consultant I talk to (including myself) is massively changing their business approach in 2026. 

2023 was about learning the new world. What is prompting. Should we ban this. What is a model. How do they work. Task force creation. Get a license for a few

author.name: Gary Sheng - The Applied AI Guy

text:
people ask me how to make money in applied AI and they're usually thinking about one thing: workflow automation.

that's real. it pays. but it's one corner of a much bigger economy.

here's a sampler of what i'm seeing:

1. workflow automation consulting (Clay, n8n, Make)
2.

Wow, after 14 years of being a software developer I would have never guessed in a million years that writing, specs, and ideas would be the bottleneck for my expressivity and output.

But here I am, 5 agent loops running in perpetuity, spending 100% of my time finding the fastest

go to linkedin. search "we're hiring" posts from companies in your target industry. 

hiring = growth. 
growth = systems breaking.

now read what role they're hiring for: "office manager" = ops chaos, "customer success" = support backlog, "sales lead" = follow-up with customers.

Mark Cuban on the next big job students should focus on:

Most companies don’t know how to implement AI, especially small businesses.

“Companies don’t understand how to implement AI right now to get a competitive advantage… learn to customize a model, walk into a company, show https://t.co/uHOlgLMdOi

There will be 2 types of companies in the future.   

Those that are great at AI, and those that used to be in business

This is our entire bet at @tenex_labs. 

AI is a generational technology, yet the journey from AI-absent to AI-native for companies of all sizes will be painful. 

Getting an individual to start using ChatGPT is easy. 

Rebuilding full processes/workflows in a company is

Add to your H1 roadmap: start an internal applied AI team.

Steal folks from existing teams to found it:

- Engineers that have seriously debated becoming a product manager

- Sales/Solutions/Field engineers that like to build

Point them at your biggest sales/marketing problem.

$10M is easy if you leave this bubble

If you know what Claude Code, clipping, or MCPs are- you're in a bubble

X is a magical corner of the internet. Take what you learn here and apply it to industries that aren't paying attention.

local in-person AI classes for corporate boomers. 

easily a $10K/mo opportunity.

rent a presentation room. run meta ads targeting 35-60 year olds. charge $500 for a 2-day hands-on workshop.

teach vibe coding, Claude, Gemini, prompting and agents.

the demand is insane.
